symptoms.symptom_name: Known hypermobile connective-tissue disorder with recurrent gut and orthostatic flares

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A known hEDS diagnosis with recurrent gastroparesis/orthostatic flares establishes the prior; the current picture is a recognisable exacerbation of the established phenotype.
A clear chronic-dysmotility background makes a new infective cause less likely than a flare of the known disorder.

symptoms.symptom_name: Intractable vomiting with inability to retain any oral fluid for ~12 h and reduced urine output

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Failure of all oral intake for 12 h with oliguria is the decisive driver of acute risk and disposition; telehealth cannot rehydrate.
Intractable nausea/vomiting from impaired gastric motility is characteristic of the visceral phenotype in flare.

symptoms.symptom_name: Postural tachycardia and near-syncope on standing, relieved by lying flat (patient-reported)

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Orthostatic intolerance / dysautonomia amplified by volume depletion
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Heart racing and near-blackout on standing with relief lying flat is the patient-obtainable signature of orthostatic intolerance; here worsened by dehydration.
Postural symptoms worsen with hypovolaemia — corroborates significant fluid loss.

symptoms.symptom_name: Generalised bloating with tight distended abdomen and cramping (not localised) pain

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Diffuse bloating and cramping from dysmotility, rather than a fixed localised focus, fits a functional flare.
Distension can occur in both; the generalised, non-localised, non-peritonitic character neither confirms nor excludes obstruction remotely.

symptoms.symptom_name: Dry mucous membranes, pale and sweaty appearance on video

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Video-observable dry lips/mucous membranes and clamminess support significant volume depletion; patient-observable, unverified.

symptoms.symptom_name: Non-bilious, non-bloody vomit with flatus passed earlier (negative acute-obstruction screen)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of faeculent/bilious vomiting and passage of flatus argue against established mechanical obstruction, though they do not fully exclude it remotely.
No blood in the vomitus argues against active GI bleeding at present.

symptoms.symptom_name: Incomplete bladder emptying / sensation of full bladder unable to void

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Incomplete emptying fits autonomic/pelvic-floor dysfunction of the phenotype and is compounded by dehydration; genuine retention needs in-person bladder assessment.

symptoms.symptom_name: Orthostatic vital signs — supine 100/60 HR110, standing 85/50 HR135 (clinician-measured)

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Orthostatic intolerance amplified by volume depletion
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Objective postural HR rise >30 bpm and systolic drop >15 mmHg — sealed clinician measurement; the AI Doctor infers circulatory compromise from the reported near-blackout on standing, not a measured BP.

symptoms.symptom_name: Distended tympanic abdomen with high-pitched sluggish bowel sounds; no peritonism/rebound/guarding; palpable bladder above symphysis

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Distension with sluggish high-pitched sounds and NO peritonism is consistent with functional dysmotility rather than a perforated/obstructed surgical abdomen — but this is a clinician hands-on finding the AI Doctor cannot obtain.
The absence of localised peritonism lowers the probability of a complicated surgical abdomen, but definitive exclusion needs in-person examination +/- imaging.

symptoms.symptom_name: Capillary blood glucose 5.5 mmol/L; no Kussmaul respirations (metabolic emergency screen)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Normal capillary glucose and absence of Kussmaul breathing argue against DKA — but these were obtained in person; telehealth cannot check glucose or ketones, so a metabolic cause is not formally excluded remotely.

symptom_clusters.target_diagnosis: Clinically significant dehydration requiring parenteral rehydration
symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: The combination of 12 h of retained-nothing vomiting, reduced urine output, dry mucous membranes and postural near-syncope is the actionable acute cluster: it is what makes home self-care unsafe and mandates urgent in-person parenteral treatment, independent of the underlying flare label.

Non-bilious non-bloody vomit, flatus passed, generalised (not localised) cramping and no peritonism together argue against a surgical abdomen — the basis for choosing urgent in-person care rather than a 000 ambulance while still safety-netting the surgical triggers.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: In-person clinical assessment with orthostatic vital signs and abdominal examination
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Same-day hands-on assessment is required to quantify orthostatic compromise, examine the abdomen (exclude peritonism/obstruction) and assess the bladder — none possible via telehealth.
Intractable vomiting with dehydration risks hypokalaemia, hyponatraemia and acute kidney injury; guides rehydration and antiemetic choice.
Assesses whether the incomplete emptying represents true retention requiring catheterisation.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Abdominal imaging (erect/supine X-ray or CT) only if the surgical question persists
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Reserved for when in-person examination cannot exclude obstruction, or if bilious/bloody vomiting or localised peritonism develop — not a first-line telehealth request.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Routine repeat outpatient endoscopy / gastric-emptying study during the acute flare
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Not indicated acutely; the acute task is rehydration and antiemesis, with dysmotility work-up deferred to outpatient specialist follow-up.
